questions.question_text: An SC associate posts a property listing on social media using only their personal name. This is:
questions.correct_answer: A violation — firm name must be included
distractors: Acceptable for personal social media
Permitted if the license number is shown
Allowed for properties under $200,000
questions.explanation: All advertising, including social media, must clearly identify the full name of the brokerage firm. Advertising without firm identification is a blind ad and is prohibited.

questions.question_text: What is a 'blind ad' in SC real estate?
questions.correct_answer: An ad that does not identify the brokerage firm
distractors: An ad for an unlisted property
An ad without a property photograph
An ad targeting a specific demographic
questions.explanation: A blind ad is any advertising that does not include the full name of the brokerage firm. Blind ads are prohibited in South Carolina — all advertising must clearly identify the firm.

questions.question_text: Under the 2024 team advertising rules, the brokerage name must appear at least what size relative to the team name?
questions.correct_answer: At least half the size of the team name
distractors: The same size as the team name
At least one-quarter the size
Any visible size is sufficient
questions.explanation: The 2024 Practice Act requires the brokerage name to appear at least half the size of the team name on any communication, and at least half as many times. Teams have until May 2027 to comply.

questions.question_text: A team at an SC brokerage calls themselves 'The Smith Group.' Under the 2024 rules, what change is needed?
questions.correct_answer: Must include the word 'team' in the name
distractors: Must include the BIC's last name
Must include all members' license numbers
No change needed if registered with the MLS
questions.explanation: The 2024 Practice Act requires team names to include the word 'team,' formatted as '— Team at — Brokerage.' 'The Smith Group' would need to become 'The Smith Team' (effective May 2027).

questions.question_text: An associate places a newspaper ad for a listing. The ad includes the associate's name and phone number but not the brokerage name. This is:
distractors: Acceptable in print media only
Permitted if the MLS number is included
Allowed for classified ads under 50 words
questions.explanation: Any ad without the brokerage firm's full name is a blind ad and is prohibited. This applies to all media — print, digital, social media, and signage.

questions.question_text: In SC, real estate commission compensation is paid directly to:
distractors: The associate who closed the deal
The client's closing attorney
The SC Real Estate Commission
questions.explanation: Compensation is always paid to the broker (BIC), not directly to the associate. The BIC then compensates the associate per their agreement. This applies to all licensed activities.

questions.question_text: Two competing brokerages agree to charge the same commission rate in their market area. This practice is:
distractors: Standard practice in competitive markets
Permitted if approved by the local MLS
Allowed if the rate is at or below 6%
questions.explanation: Commission rates must be freely negotiable. Brokerages agreeing on rates is price-fixing, which violates federal antitrust laws (Sherman Act). Commission rates are never fixed by law or agreement.

questions.question_text: A homeowner offers to pay their neighbor $500 for referring a buyer. The neighbor is unlicensed. Under SC law:
questions.correct_answer: Unlicensed persons cannot receive referral fees
distractors: This is allowed for one-time referrals
This is permitted for amounts under $1,000
This is legal if the neighbor discloses it
questions.explanation: Unlicensed persons may not receive compensation for performing licensed activities, including referrals that lead to transactions. Only licensed brokers may receive real estate compensation.

questions.question_text: An associate successfully closes a transaction. The cooperating brokerage sends a commission check directly to the associate. This is:
questions.correct_answer: Improper — payment must go to the BIC
distractors: Standard practice for cooperating brokers
Acceptable if the BIC authorized it verbally
Permitted for transactions over $500,000
questions.explanation: All compensation must flow through the BIC, not directly to the associate. Even in cooperating broker situations, the commission goes from one BIC to another, then to the associate.

questions.question_text: Under SC law, commission rates charged by brokerages are:
questions.correct_answer: Freely negotiable between broker and client
distractors: Set by the SCREC annually
Standardized at 6% statewide
Fixed by local MLS organizations
questions.explanation: Commission rates in SC are always freely negotiable. No entity — SCREC, MLS, or association — may set or fix commission rates. Any agreement to fix rates violates antitrust law.

questions.question_text: Under the 2024 Practice Act, 'wholesaling' in SC real estate means:
questions.correct_answer: Marketing contracted property before taking ownership
distractors: Buying multiple properties at a bulk discount
Selling properties below market value quickly
Purchasing foreclosed properties at public auction
questions.explanation: Wholesaling means having a contractual interest in purchasing residential real estate, then marketing the property for sale to a different buyer before taking legal ownership. This is prohibited for licensees.

questions.question_text: A licensee holds a purchase contract on a home and markets the home itself to other buyers before closing. Under the 2024 Act, this is:
distractors: Permitted as standard business practice
Allowed if the seller consents in writing
Legal if the licensee discloses their interest
questions.explanation: Marketing the underlying real property before taking legal ownership is wholesaling and is prohibited. However, marketing the contractual position itself (contract assignment) is permitted.

questions.question_text: What is the key distinction between prohibited wholesaling and permitted contract assignment in SC?
questions.correct_answer: Assignments market the contract, not the property
distractors: Assignments require SCREC pre-approval
Wholesaling involves commercial properties only
Assignments are limited to investors only
questions.explanation: Contract assignments are permitted — you may market your contractual position. The prohibition is on marketing the underlying real property itself before taking legal ownership. The distinction is what is being marketed.

questions.question_text: An unlicensed investor asks a licensee to help market a property the investor has under contract but does not own. The licensee should:
questions.correct_answer: Decline — assisting in wholesaling is prohibited
distractors: Proceed if the investor signs a listing agreement
Help if the property is commercial, not residential
Assist if the investor pays double the commission
questions.explanation: All brokerages and licensees cannot engage in, represent others in, or assist others in wholesaling. Helping an investor market property they don't own is grounds for discipline under the 2024 Act.

questions.question_text: The 2024 wholesaling prohibition applies to which type of real estate?
distractors: All real estate including commercial
Only single-family homes
Only properties under $500,000
questions.explanation: The wholesaling definition specifically refers to residential real estate. A person with a contractual interest in purchasing residential real estate cannot market it to other buyers before taking ownership.
